reasoning: B wins on reasoning architecture: every option carries an explicit falsifier, and the verdict names the single load-bearing uncertainty (regulatory rigidity vs. genuine absence of nearby population/transit) and designs the pilot to discriminate between them within a year, which is the cleanest application of falsification and the strongest guard against confirmation bias. It also reframes the goal to a measurable stock (presence-hours per block) and pushes strong cheap levers over weak ones, with Goodhart and high-modernist self-checks. D is a close second and has the most genuinely non-obvious insight (vacancy is rational for landlords protecting headline valuation/loan covenants, fixed by a city master-lease that shields the valuation), plus explicit highest-uncertainty naming, but it is slightly less systematic on per-option falsification. A is excellent and uniquely offers a numeric confidence (~75%) with named key uncertainties (conversion cost, legal challenge, deeper crime/jobs causes), but it leans on a similar residential-anchor-activation triad with less original mechanism than D and less falsification structure than B. C is solid, with a sharp coordination-failure/first-mover reframing and an inversion device, but it is the weakest on calibration (no probabilities, no falsifiers, fewest named uncertainties), so it ranks last.
